title: Topic 113: The Sunk Cost Fallacy in Gaming: Why Past Spending Predicts Future Spending
content:
_**Topic 113: The Sunk Cost Fallacy in Gaming: Why Past Spending Predicts Future Spending**_

**ABSTRACT**

Welcome, dear reader, to the peculiar world of the modern gamer, a creature of such paradoxical dedication that they might be mistaken for a digital Sisyphus, forever pushing a boulder of pixels up a virtual hill. This paper delves into the psychological gremlin that whispers sweet nothings into the ear of this gamer: the Sunk Cost Fallacy. It’s the little voice that insists, “You’ve already invested 500 hours and a sum of money that could have purchased a small, artisanal cheese farm into this game. Quitting now would be… illogical.” With the precision of a surgeon and the wit of a court jester, this paper will dissect this phenomenon, exploring how game designers, the mischievous architects of our digital playgrounds, have mastered the art of turning our own irrationality into a remarkably profitable revenue stream. We promise that the time you invest in reading this paper will yield a far greater return than one more hour spent grinding for a slightly shinier virtual sword. Probably.

**INTRODUCTION**

Picture the scene: a dimly lit room, the only illumination emanating from a glowing screen. A lone figure, eyes bloodshot and twitching, mutters the gamer’s mantra: “Just one more level.” The irony is, they haven’t truly *enjoyed* this particular game in months. It has become a chore, a second job with no pay and a much higher risk of carpal tunnel syndrome. This is not, as one might assume, a simple lack of willpower. It is a textbook case of a cognitive bias, a glitch in the human operating system known as the Sunk Cost Fallacy.

In its simplest form, the Sunk Cost Fallacy is the reason you force yourself to finish a truly disgusting, overpriced meal simply because you’ve already paid for it. It’s the nagging feeling that you must get your money’s worth, even if the “worth” is a mouthful of something that tastes like regret. In the digital realm of gaming, this fallacy finds its most fertile ground. The time, the effort, and, increasingly, the money that players pour into a game create a powerful illusion of investment, a psychological anchor that makes the prospect of abandonment feel like a profound personal failure.

This paper will embark on a journey into the heart of this digital dilemma. We will begin in the hallowed halls of academia, reviewing the foundational research on this peculiar human quirk. From there, we will venture into the digital trenches, exploring the myriad ways in which game designers have weaponized this fallacy, transforming it into a cornerstone of modern game design. We will examine real-world examples, from the sprawling landscapes of *World of Warcraft* to the candy-coated battlefields of mobile gaming, and hear from the players themselves, the willing and unwilling victims of their own cognitive biases. Finally, we will discuss the broader implications of this phenomenon, not just for the future of gaming, but for our understanding of consumer behavior in an increasingly digital world. So, grab your favorite beverage, settle into your gaming chair, and prepare to level up your understanding of the Sunk Cost Fallacy. We promise there will be no microtransactions along the way.

**LITERATURE REVIEW**

To understand the modern gamer’s plight, we must first pay our respects to the godfathers of irrationality, Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky. In the 1970s, these intrepid psychologists embarked on a safari into the wilds of the human mind and returned with a veritable menagerie of cognitive biases, those delightful little glitches in our thinking that lead us to make decisions that are, to put it mildly, bonkers [1]. Among their discoveries was the Sunk Cost Fallacy, a concept that, at its core, is quite simple: we are suckers for a bad investment. The more we pour into something, whether it be time, money, or emotional energy, the more we feel compelled to see it through, even when it’s clear that the ship is not only sinking but has, in fact, already reached the bottom of the ocean and is being repurposed as a coral reef.

Early research into this phenomenon was, as is often the case in academia, rather dry. Friedman et al. (2007), for instance, had subjects play a computer game where they had to decide whether to keep digging for a treasure, a study that sounds suspiciously like a game of academic Minecraft [2]. Their findings, while groundbreaking, essentially confirmed what every person who has ever overeaten at an all-you-can-eat buffet already knows: we hate to feel like we’ve wasted our resources. The study by Skognes and Noste (2020) further solidified this, demonstrating a clear link between sunk costs and increased risk-taking behavior in a computer game setting [3]. It seems that once we’ve thrown a few coins into the digital wishing well, we’re more than willing to toss in a few more, just in case a unicorn pops out.

But how does this translate to the complex, multifaceted world of modern gaming? Toh (2021) provides a crucial bridge, observing the sunk cost effect in the context of video games and even suggesting their pedagogical potential [4]. One can only imagine a future where high school economics is taught through the lens of *Fortnite* skins and *League of Legends* champions. The connection becomes even clearer when we introduce the concept of **Loss Aversion**, another of Kahneman and Tversky’s greatest hits. As the article from Touro University Worldwide so aptly puts it, we are more motivated to avoid losses than to achieve gains [5]. In the gamer’s psyche, this translates to a desperate need to protect their “investment.” The prospect of quitting a game and “losing” thousands of hours of progress, a finely honed character, and a collection of rare digital hats is, for many, a more terrifying prospect than facing the final boss on the highest difficulty setting.

This psychological cocktail is further potentized by what is known as **Escalation of Commitment**, a close cousin of the Sunk Cost Fallacy. It’s the voice that says, “I’ve already come this far, I can’t turn back now!” This is the fuel that powers the late-night gaming sessions, the “one more quest” that turns into an all-nighter. As we will see, game designers have become masters of creating gameplay loops that not only encourage but demand this kind of escalating commitment, turning what should be a leisure activity into a relentless march towards a goal that is always just out of reach.

**ANALYSIS: INSIDE THE DIGITAL CASINO**

If the Sunk Cost Fallacy is the ghost in our cognitive machine, then game designers are the paranormal investigators who have learned to harness its power. In the golden age of gaming, the goal was simple: create a fun and engaging experience that people would be willing to pay for upfront. But in the modern era of “games as a service,” the business model has shifted. The goal is no longer just to create a game, but to create a habitat, a digital ecosystem designed to keep players engaged and, more importantly, spending. This is where the Sunk Cost Fallacy transforms from a quirky cognitive bias into a powerful tool for monetization.

Let us dissect the unholy trinity of modern game design, the three pillars upon which the temple of sunk cost is built:

First, we have **microtransactions**, the seemingly innocuous purchases that are the bread and butter of the free-to-play model. A dollar here for a new character skin, a few more for a pack of in-game currency. Each individual purchase is small enough to feel insignificant, a mere drop in the digital bucket. But these drops accumulate, and before the player knows it, they have invested a sum of money that could have funded a weekend getaway to a place with actual, tangible sunshine. This financial investment, however small it may seem at the time, becomes a powerful psychological anchor. The player, having spent real money on the game, now feels a subconscious obligation to continue playing, to “get their money’s worth.”

Next, we have the clever obfuscation of **in-game currencies**. By converting real money into a whimsical currency of “gems,” “coins,” or the ever-popular “V-Bucks,” game designers create a psychological buffer between the player and their wallet. Spending 1000 V-Bucks on a new dance move feels far less significant than spending $10. This abstraction of value makes it easier for players to part with their money, further deepening the financial sunk cost and strengthening the chains that bind them to the game.

Finally, we have the pièce de résistance, the unholy marriage of the Sunk Cost Fallacy and a Las Vegas slot machine: the **loot box**, or “gacha” mechanic. Here, players spend money not for a specific item, but for the *chance* to receive a rare and desirable item. This introduces the element of gambling into the equation. The more a player spends without receiving the coveted prize, the more they feel compelled to keep spending. Each failed attempt is not just a loss of money, but an increase in the sunk cost, an escalation of commitment that can lead to staggering levels of spending. It is a system that is so effective, and so potentially predatory, that it has drawn the attention of regulators worldwide.

But the most significant sunk cost for many gamers is not money, but time. The thousands of hours spent mastering a game’s mechanics, the countless nights dedicated to grinding for better gear, the emotional energy invested in a character’s journey – this is a currency that cannot be refunded. This temporal investment creates a powerful sense of ownership and attachment, a feeling that to quit the game would be to invalidate all of that time and effort. The gameplay loops in many modern games are, in essence, a digital hamster wheel. The player runs and runs, fueled by the promise of a slightly better reward, a slightly higher rank, a slightly shinier piece of armor. The Sunk Cost Fallacy is the psychological grease that keeps the wheel turning, ensuring that the player never questions why they are running in the first place.

**REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: TALES FROM THE CRYPT (OF FORGOTTEN GAMES)**

To truly appreciate the masterful application of the Sunk Cost Fallacy, we must venture into the digital wild and observe it in its natural habitat. The gaming world is littered with the ghosts of abandoned accounts and forgotten characters, each a testament to the power of this cognitive bias.

Our first stop is the sprawling graveyard of the MMORPG, a genre that is practically built on the foundation of sunk costs. Consider *World of Warcraft*, the undisputed king of the genre. For over a decade, it has held millions of players in its thrall, not just through its engaging gameplay, but through the sheer weight of their investment. Players who have spent years, and in some cases, decades, cultivating their characters, amassing fortunes in virtual gold, and forging bonds with fellow guild members find it incredibly difficult to walk away. As one commenter on a Reddit thread lamented, quitting would feel like “throwing away a part of my life” [6]. This sentiment is echoed across countless forums and social media platforms, a chorus of players bound to a game not by joy, but by a sense of obligation to their past selves. Then there is the case of *ArcheAge*, a game that took the concept of loss aversion to a whole new level with its land ownership system. Players could claim a plot of land and build a house, but only if they maintained a premium subscription to pay the in-game taxes. Failure to do so would result in the demolition of their virtual home, a tangible and emotionally charged loss that kept many players paying long after the initial excitement had worn off.

Next, we descend into the candy-coated hellscape of mobile gaming, a realm where the Sunk Cost Fallacy is not just a feature, but the entire business model. Games like *Clash of Clans* and *Rise of Kingdoms* are masters of the “pay-to-skip” mechanic. They are designed around long, tedious wait times that can be conveniently bypassed with a small payment. Each payment, each skipped timer, is another coin in the sunk cost jar, another reason to keep playing and protect the “investment.” But the true apex predator of the mobile world is the gacha game. Titles like *Genshin Impact* and *Fate/Grand Order* have perfected the art of the random-chance purchase, luring players in with the promise of powerful characters and weapons, and then bleeding them dry with abysmal drop rates. The internet is rife with horror stories of players who have spent thousands, and in some cases, tens of thousands of dollars in pursuit of a single, elusive JPEG. Each failed attempt only deepens the sunk cost, creating a vicious cycle of spending and regret.

Finally, we have the curious case of the cosmetic compulsion. In games like *League of Legends* and *Fortnite*, the vast majority of microtransactions are for purely cosmetic items: character skins, dance moves, and weapon charms that have absolutely no impact on gameplay. And yet, players spend billions of dollars on them every year. Why? Because these cosmetic items are a form of self-expression, a way to personalize one’s digital avatar. But they are also a form of investment. A player who has spent hundreds of dollars on a collection of rare skins is far less likely to quit the game, even if they are no longer enjoying the core gameplay. The skins become a sunk cost, a digital wardrobe that is too valuable to abandon.

**DISCUSSION: SO, WHAT’S THE BIG DEAL?**

It is tempting to dismiss the Sunk Cost Fallacy in gaming as a trivial matter, a quirky feature of a niche hobby. After all, what’s the harm in a few wasted hours or a handful of dollars spent on virtual trinkets? But to do so would be to miss the bigger picture. The digital casinos of the gaming world are not an isolated phenomenon; they are a microcosm of our broader consumer culture, a canary in the coal mine of our increasingly irrational economic behavior. If we are willing to spend real money to save a virtual farm from withering, what does that say about our other purchasing decisions? The same psychological hooks that keep us glued to our screens are at play when we upgrade our smartphones every year, when we buy clothes we don’t need, and when we hold onto failing investments in the vain hope that they will one day recover.

This raises a crucial ethical question: where do we draw the line between good game design and predatory psychological manipulation? Is it fair for game developers to so skillfully and deliberately exploit the known bugs in our cognitive wiring for financial gain? Are they artists creating immersive and engaging experiences, or are they casino operators in disguise, meticulously crafting systems designed to separate us from our money? The answer, as is often the case, lies in a murky gray area. There is nothing inherently wrong with creating a compelling and rewarding gameplay loop. But when that loop is designed to be just frustrating enough to encourage spending, when the rewards are doled out on a variable-ratio schedule that mimics a slot machine, and when the entire experience is built around the principle of sunk cost, we have to wonder if a line has been crossed.

Ultimately, the Sunk Cost Fallacy in gaming holds up a mirror to ourselves. It reflects our own irrationality, our own susceptibility to the siren song of bad investments. The same cognitive bias that keeps us playing a game we no longer enjoy is the same one that keeps us in dead-end jobs, in failing relationships, and in that online course we paid for but have no intention of finishing. But there is hope. The first step to conquering any cognitive bias is awareness. By understanding the psychological forces at play, we can begin to reclaim our agency. For the gamer, this means learning to ask a simple but powerful question: “Am I having fun *right now*?” If the answer is no, then it may be time to walk away, regardless of how many hours have been invested or how much money has been spent. The past is a sunk cost, and the only thing that truly matters is the present.

**CONCLUSION: THE ‘GAME OVER’ SCREEN**

In the grand, pixelated tapestry of human irrationality, the Sunk Cost Fallacy in gaming stands out as a particularly vibrant thread. We have journeyed from the hallowed halls of behavioral economics to the digital trenches of modern gaming, and the conclusion is clear: our brains are, to put it mildly, weird. We are wired to cling to our past investments, whether they be of time, money, or emotion, with the tenacity of a barnacle on a battleship. Game designers, in their infinite wisdom and pursuit of profit, have learned to exploit this quirk with a masterful touch, creating a new generation of games that are as much about psychological manipulation as they are about entertainment.

But this is not a story of doom and gloom. It is a story of awareness. The true “final boss” for the modern gamer is not a fire-breathing dragon or a universe-conquering alien, but their own irrational brain. By understanding the Sunk Cost Fallacy, by recognizing the psychological hooks that are so cleverly embedded in the games we play, we can begin to fight back. We can learn to value our present enjoyment over our past investments. We can reclaim our hobbies from the clutches of cognitive bias and remember the simple, joyful reason we started playing in the first place: to have fun. So, the next time you find yourself grinding for hours in a game you no longer enjoy, remember the words of this humble paper. The ‘game over’ screen is not a failure; it is an opportunity to start a new game, one that you might actually enjoy.
references:
[1] Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. *Econometrica*, *47*(2), 263–291.
[2] Friedman, D., Pommerenke, K., Lukose, R., Milam, G., & Huberman, B. A. (2007). Searching for the sunk cost fallacy. *Experimental Economics*, *10*(1), 79–104.
[3] Skognes, M. T., & Noste, H. S. K. (2020). *The Sunk Cost Fallacy and Risk-Taking Behaviour. Evidence from a computer game experiment*. Munin.
[4] Toh, W. (2021). The economics of decision-making in video games. *Game Studies*, *21*(3).
[5] Touro University Worldwide. (2016, February 25). *Insert More Coins | The Psychology Behind Microtransactions*. https://www.tuw.edu/psychology/psychology-behind-microtransactions/
[6] Reddit. (2017, December 18). *The Sunk Cost Fallacy, and why you are guilty of it*. https://www.reddit.com/r/MMORPG/comments/7knsyt/the_sunk_cost_fallacy_and_why_you_are_guilty_of_it/
